title: AI Memory Systems
lesson:
Here is an uncomfortable truth about every large language model: it has amnesia. The moment a request finishes, the model forgets everything. It does not secretly remember you between chats. The only thing it ever "knows" in a conversation is whatever text you hand it that turn, packed into its context window (the fixed-size workspace of tokens it can read at once). When that text scrolls off, it's gone. An AI memory system is the scaffolding we build around the model to give it the feeling of remembering — by storing facts outside the model and feeding the right ones back in at the right moment.

Why the model can't just "remember"

Think of the model as a brilliant consultant with no long-term memory at all — like the lead in the film Memento. Every meeting, they walk in fresh. To work with them, you keep a notebook of important facts and slide the relevant page across the table before each meeting. The consultant reads it, sounds like they remember you, then forgets again the second they leave.

The memory lives in your notebook, not in their head. That notebook is the memory system. The model is still amnesiac; we just got very good at handing it the right page.

Three layers of memory

Most real systems blend three kinds, just like a person does:

  • Working memory — the current conversation, held directly in the context window. Fast, but small and temporary, like the sticky notes on your desk right now.
  • Short-term / session memory — a running summary of the chat so far, so a long conversation doesn't blow past the context limit. Older turns get squeezed into a compact recap.
  • Long-term memory — durable facts kept in a database across sessions: your name, your preferences, decisions made last week. This is where RAG (retrieval-augmented generation) machinery comes in.

How long-term memory actually works

Long-term memory is mostly RAG pointed at you instead of at documents. When something worth remembering appears ("I'm vegetarian", "my project ships in March"), the system saves it as a short note. To recall it later, the note is turned into an embedding — a list of numbers capturing its meaning — and stored in a vector database.

On a new message, the system embeds the message too and runs a semantic search for the closest stored notes, then drops the best matches into the prompt before the model answers. This is the same retrieval pipeline used for documents, with one twist: memory is written as well as read.

A second analogy helps. A memory system is a library with a very busy librarian. The shelves (the vector database) hold thousands of note cards. You ask a question; the librarian doesn't dump every card on you — they fetch the handful actually about your topic and lay them on the desk (the context window). The model only ever reads what's on the desk.

# Pseudocode for a tiny long-term memory loop
def respond(user_msg, memory_db, llm):
    # 1. RECALL: find notes related to this message
    hits = memory_db.search(embed(user_msg), top_k=4)
    notes = "\n".join(h.text for h in hits)

    # 2. ANSWER: give the model the recalled notes + the message
    prompt = f"Known about user:\n{notes}\n\nUser: {user_msg}"
    reply = llm.generate(prompt)

    # 3. WRITE: save anything new worth remembering
    if fact := extract_durable_fact(user_msg, reply):
        memory_db.add(text=fact, vector=embed(fact))
    return reply

Three steps: recall, answer, write. That loop is the heart of nearly every memory system, from a hobby chatbot to ChatGPT's memory feature to frameworks like Mem0, Letta (formerly MemGPT), or LangGraph's memory stores.

The hard part: deciding what to keep and forget

Saving everything is a trap. Store every message and recall gets noisy — the librarian buries the useful card under junk, and you waste context budget. Good systems are picky: they extract crisp facts, merge duplicates, update stale ones ("actually I moved to Berlin"), and let unused memories fade. A common pattern is summarize-and-discard: roll ten chatty turns into one tidy fact, then drop the raw turns.

User said over time:        Stored memory (deduped):
"I love spicy food"     ->  diet: enjoys spicy food
"I'm vegetarian btw"    ->  diet: vegetarian (no meat)
"actually pescatarian"  ->  diet: pescatarian (fish ok)  [updated]

Notice the third line overwrites the second. Memory isn't an append-only log; it's a curated, living profile. This is also why memory raises real privacy duties: you are now keeping personal data, so let users see it, edit it, and delete it.

Where does this matter? A coding agent that remembers your stack and conventions. A support bot that recalls a customer's past tickets. A tutor that tracks what you've already mastered. In all of them the model stays amnesiac — the product feels like it remembers because the memory system quietly slid the right page across the desk.
mentalModel: The model is an amnesiac genius. The memory system is the notebook you keep for it: you jot down durable facts, and before each turn you slide only the relevant page across the desk. The remembering lives in the notebook, never in the model's head.
takeaways: LLMs are amnesiac — memory lives in an external store, not the model.
The core loop is recall → answer → write every turn.
Curate ruthlessly: extract facts, dedupe, update, and respect privacy.
mistakes: Believing the model itself remembers across chats. It doesn't — every request starts blank. Any 'memory' is text your system fetched from a store and re-injected into the prompt. Forget to fetch and re-inject, and the model knows nothing about last week.

Saving every message verbatim. A giant pile of raw turns makes recall noisy and expensive: the right fact gets buried, and you burn context-window budget on filler. Extract concise facts and discard the chatter.

Never updating or deleting stale facts. If memory is append-only, contradictions accumulate ('lives in London' and 'lives in Berlin' both surface) and the model gets confused. Treat memory as a living profile you dedupe and overwrite, not a logbook.

Confusing a long context window with memory. A bigger window holds more current text, but it still empties when the request ends. Persistence needs an external store; a long window alone is just a bigger desk, not a filing cabinet.

Stuffing all stored memories into the prompt 'just in case'. That defeats the point — you re-create the noise and cost you were avoiding. Retrieve only the top few relevant notes with semantic search, exactly like good RAG.

Ignoring privacy. A memory system is a database of personal facts about real people. If users can't view, edit, or delete what's stored about them, you have a compliance and trust problem, not a feature.
exercise:
Build a tiny memory loop in any language. Keep an in-memory list of 'notes' (plain strings). Write three functions: recall(msg) that returns the notes whose words overlap most with the message (a crude stand-in for semantic search), answer(msg, notes) that just prints the message plus the recalled notes, and write(msg) that saves any sentence starting with 'remember' or 'I am' as a new note. Run a short scripted chat: tell it 'I am vegetarian', ask 'what should I cook?', and confirm the vegetarian note is recalled and shown before the answer.

Stretch goal: Add updating. When a new note shares a category prefix with an old one (e.g. both start 'diet:'), overwrite the old note instead of appending — then feed it 'I am pescatarian now' and prove the vegetarian note is replaced, not duplicated.
solution: The core loop is recall → answer → write. A workable recall: lowercase everything, split into words, score each note by how many words it shares with the message, and return the top one or two. write checks for trigger phrases and appends the cleaned sentence as a note. For the stretch goal, give each note a category (the text before the first colon, or inferred from a keyword like 'diet'); before appending, scan for an existing note with the same category and replace it in place. After 'I am pescatarian now' your store should hold one diet note, not two — that single-overwrite behavior is the difference between a curated profile and a noisy logbook. The same idea scales up: swap the word-overlap score for real embeddings in a vector database and the dedupe rule for an LLM that decides whether two facts conflict.
summary: LLMs are amnesiac — they only know the text in their context window this turn. A memory system is the scaffolding around the model that fakes remembering: it stores facts in an external database and feeds the relevant ones back into the prompt. The loop is recall, answer, write, and it spans working memory (the live chat), short-term memory (a running summary), and long-term memory (durable facts via RAG and a vector database). The real skill is curation — extract crisp facts, update and dedupe them, retrieve only the few that matter, and respect privacy.

diagram.caption: A new message recalls matching notes from the vector DB, the amnesiac model answers, then any new fact is written back into the store.

quiz.q: A user told your chatbot 'I'm allergic to peanuts' last week. They start a brand-new chat today and ask for a snack idea. Why might the bot still know about the allergy?
options: The model permanently learned the fact during last week's chat
A memory system retrieved the stored note and injected it into today's prompt
Longer context windows automatically carry facts between sessions
The model has hidden long-term memory that activates after a week
quiz.explain: The model is amnesiac and learned nothing. The bot 'remembers' only because the memory system fetched the saved note and added it to the new prompt.

quiz.q: Which of these is the recall–answer–write loop doing in the WRITE step?
options: Searching the vector database for notes relevant to the message
Sending the recalled notes plus the message to the model
Saving any new durable fact from the exchange back into the store
Summarizing the entire conversation into the context window
quiz.explain: Write is the persistence step: it extracts anything worth keeping and adds it to the store. Searching is recall and sending to the model is answer.

quiz.q: Your team upgrades to a model with a much larger context window and declares 'now we have real memory.' What's wrong with that claim?
options: A larger window is slower, so it cancels out any memory benefit
The window still empties when the request ends; persistence needs an external store
Large windows can't hold conversation history, only documents
Nothing — a big enough context window is the same as a memory system
quiz.explain: A bigger window is just a bigger desk for the current turn. Once the request finishes the text is gone, so durable memory still requires an external database.

quiz.q: A user says 'I'm vegetarian', then weeks later says 'actually I eat fish now.' What should a well-designed memory system do?
options: Keep both notes so it has the full history available
Overwrite or update the diet note so it reflects the current fact
Delete all diet notes and stop tracking diet entirely
Inject both notes every turn and let the model decide
quiz.explain: Memory is a curated living profile, not an append-only log. Keeping both notes creates contradictions; updating to the latest fact keeps recall clean and correct.

keyTerms.def: The scaffolding built around an LLM that stores facts outside the model and feeds the relevant ones back into the prompt, creating the illusion that the model remembers.
The current conversation held directly in the context window. Fast and immediate, but small and wiped when the request ends.
A running, compact summary of the chat so far, used so a long conversation doesn't overflow the context window.
Durable facts kept in an external database across sessions (names, preferences, past decisions), recalled on demand via retrieval.
The three-step heart of a memory system: search the store for relevant notes, answer using them, then save anything new worth keeping.
Rolling many raw conversation turns into one concise fact and dropping the originals, to keep memory small and recall clean.
The work of extracting crisp facts, merging duplicates, overwriting stale ones, and letting unused memories fade, so the store stays a living profile rather than a noisy log.
Finding stored notes by meaning rather than exact words, by comparing the embedding of the new message to the embeddings of saved notes.
